narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 96% chance that you would not pass. This high predicted risk of failure automatically triggers a rejection to ensure the program's overall success rates. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group and significantly pushed the prediction toward expecting a bar failure, unlike the much higher mid-level deciles typical of students who passed. Next in influence was your LSAT score of 24, well below the average of about 37 for those who succeeded on the bar, further tilting the odds against you. Your Year 1 decile ranking in the lowest group also contributed notably in the same direction, reinforcing concerns about early academic performance compared to passers who averaged around the sixth decile. While your middle family income aligns with over a third of successful students and your choice to study full-time matches nearly all of them, these did not outweigh the top concerns from your grades and test score. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.6, lower than the 3.24 average for bar passers, played a supporting role but was less decisive here. In essence, your profile stood out as riskier than typical admitted students, whose stronger early rankings, higher LSATs, and solid GPAs pointed to bar success. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's a data-driven call to protect the school's outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly, strengthening your high school decile performance through additional coursework or programs, and building a more competitive undergraduate record if possible. Gathering letters from mentors who can highlight your resilience could also help in reapplying or appealing. With targeted improvements, you can shift your profile closer to successful applicants and try again.
